title: Heat Engines & Refrigerators
subtitle: Heat engines and refrigerators for Class 11 Physics (NCERT) — efficiency, COP

blocks.heading: Performance measures:
blocks.body:
A heat engine operates in a cycle, taking heat from a hot reservoir, converting part into work, and rejecting the rest to a cold reservoir.
A refrigerator (or heat pump) does the reverse: it uses work to move heat from cold to hot.
Engine: efficiency \eta = W/Q_H
Refrigerator: coefficient of performance COP = Q_C/W
Heat pump: COP = Q_H/W
No real engine achieves 100% efficiency — the second law forbids it.

blocks.heading: Heat engine:
blocks.body:
Refrigerator COP:
Heat pump COP:
Carnot efficiency (maximum possible for given T_H, T_C):
No real engine can exceed Carnot efficiency operating between the same temperatures.

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: Coal-fired power station: A steam turbine takes heat from burning coal (~550°C = 823 K) and rejects heat to a river or cooling tower (~30°C = 303 K). Maximum (Carnot) efficiency ≈ 1 − 303/823 ≈ 63%. Actual efficiency is ~35–40% due to irreversibilities. The remaining heat goes into the river or atmosphere — that is why power stations need cooling towers.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Consider a car engine: it burns petrol (releases ~2.5 MJ/litre), operates between ~2000 K (combustion) and ~400 K (exhaust). Carnot efficiency would be 1 − 400/2000 = 80%. Real petrol engines achieve ~20–30%. Where does the rest go — exhaust heat, engine friction, cooling system?

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: An air conditioner has COP = 3. For every 1 unit of electrical energy consumed, it pumps 3 units of heat from indoors to outdoors. The total heat going outside is 1 + 3 = 4 units. So an air conditioner actually heats the city more than it cools your room — the waste heat from all the air conditioners in a city contributes to the urban heat island effect.
